sentences.base_language: One morning, Li Wei woke up with a headache and a stomachache, and felt very tired.
sentences.target_language: 一天早晨，李伟醒来时头疼、肚子疼，感觉非常疲倦。
sentences.romanized: Yī tiān zǎochen, Lǐ Wěi xǐng lái shí tóuténg, dùziténg, gǎnjué fēicháng píjuàn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**头疼** (tóuténg) = headache, lit. 'head hurts' — the pattern **[body part] + 疼** expresses pain: 肚子**疼** (stomachache), 背**疼** (backache), 腿**疼** (leg pain). **疼** (téng) = to ache/hurt. Compare **痛** (tòng), which is more intense or formal: 头痛 (headache — slightly more medical).
**醒来时** = when [he] woke up — **醒来** (xǐng lái) = wake up (become awake). The 时 (shí, time/when) after a verb phrase creates a temporal clause: 吃饭**时** (while eating), 到家**时** (when arriving home). A very natural Chinese narrative construction.

sentences.base_language: Lately he had been working a lot and wasn't resting properly.
sentences.target_language: 最近他工作太多，没有好好休息。
sentences.romanized: Zuìjìn tā gōngzuò tài duō, méiyǒu hǎohǎo xiūxi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**太 + adjective/verb** = too ~: 工作太**多** (worked too much), 吃得太**快** (eating too fast). **太** expresses excess — often carries a slightly negative or surprising connotation: 这个太贵了 (this is too expensive!). With a following 了, it can soften to 'really quite': 太好了！(that's great!)
**好好** = properly / to one's heart's content — reduplication of **好** (hǎo, good) creates an adverb meaning 'thoroughly/properly': 好好休息 (rest properly), 好好想想 (think it over carefully). Adjective reduplication in Chinese often adds an affectionate or emphatic quality.

sentences.base_language: He decided to go to the hospital near his home.
sentences.target_language: 他决定去家附近的医院。
sentences.romanized: Tā juédìng qù jiā fùjìn de yīyuàn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**决定 + verb** = decide to do: 决定**去** (decided to go), 决定**留下** (decided to stay). **决定** as a verb takes the following verb phrase directly — no preposition needed, unlike English 'decide **to**.' Compare: **打算** (dǎsuàn, plan to) and **准备** (zhǔnbèi, prepare to/plan to) follow the same pattern.
**家附近的医院** — the **的** (de) structure: [modifier] + **的** + noun. Here **家附近** (near the home) modifies **医院**: the hospital that is near home. The modifier precedes the noun in Chinese, connected by **的**: 我买的书 (the book I bought), 他住的地方 (the place he lives).

sentences.base_language: He washed his face and got dressed, but felt too weak to eat breakfast.
sentences.target_language: 他洗了脸，穿好了衣服，但感觉太虚弱，吃不下早饭。
sentences.romanized: Tā xǐle liǎn, chuān hǎo le yīfu, dàn gǎnjué tài xūruò, chī bu xià zǎofàn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**穿好了** — resultative complement: **穿** (chuān, to put on/wear) + **好** (hǎo, well/successfully) = successfully put on. Resultative complements show the outcome of an action: 写**完** (finish writing), 听**懂** (understand by hearing), 做**好** (do successfully). The 了 marks the completed result.
**吃不下** = unable to eat (not able to get food down) — **V + 不 + complement** is the potential negative: 吃**不**下 (can't eat), 喝**不**下 (can't drink), 睡**不**着 (can't sleep). The positive potential: 吃**得**下 (can eat), 睡**得**着 (can sleep). A very common and expressive Chinese pattern.

sentences.base_language: The hospital was two subway stops from his apartment.
sentences.target_language: 医院离他的公寓有两站地铁的距离。
sentences.romanized: Yīyuàn lí tā de gōngyù yǒu liǎng zhàn dìtiě de jùlí.
sentences.grammarNote:
**离** = from / away from (distance): 医院**离**公寓 (the hospital from the apartment). **离** expresses spatial or temporal distance: 这里**离**学校很近 (here is close to the school), 还**离**考试一周 (still a week from the exam). Compare **从** (cóng, from — used for origin/starting point of movement): **从**家到医院 (from home to the hospital).
**两站地铁** = two subway stops — **站** (zhàn) is both 'station' and 'stop': 两**站**地铁 (two subway stops), 地铁**站** (subway station). Chinese measure words: **两站** uses the implied measure word **站**; in counting transit stops, 站 serves as both noun and measure word.

sentences.base_language: At the registration desk, a nurse asked him: "What seems to be the problem?"
sentences.target_language: 在挂号处，一位护士问他：“您哪里不舒服？”
sentences.romanized: Zài guàhào chù, yī wèi hùshi wèn tā: “Nín nǎlǐ bù shūfu?”
sentences.grammarNote:
**挂号处** (guàhào chù) = registration desk — **挂号** (guàhào) = to register (for a hospital appointment): 先**挂号**再看病 (register first, then see the doctor). A key term for Chinese hospitals where registration is required before seeing a doctor. **处** (chù) = place/section: 入口**处** (entrance), 服务**处** (service desk).
**哪里不舒服** = where is the problem? lit. 'where is it uncomfortable?' — Chinese uses this phrase for medical symptoms: **哪里**不舒服? (where does it hurt?). **不舒服** = unwell/uncomfortable: 我身体**不舒服** (I'm not feeling well). **您** (nín) is the polite/formal 'you' — used with strangers, elders, and in service contexts.

sentences.base_language: He replied: "My head and stomach hurt, and I feel completely exhausted."
sentences.target_language: 他回答说：“我头疼，肚子也疼，而且感觉筋疲力尽。”
sentences.romanized: Tā huídá shuō: “Wǒ tóuténg, dùzi yě téng, érqiě gǎnjué jīnpí lìjìn.”
sentences.grammarNote:
**回答说** = replied saying — **说** (shuō) is regularly added after verbs of speech before a quotation: 他说 (he said), 她回答**说** (she replied saying), 老师解释**说** (the teacher explained). This pattern is very natural in Chinese narration.
**筋疲力尽** (jīnpí lìjìn) = utterly exhausted — a four-character idiom (成语, chéngyǔ): lit. 'muscles tired, strength exhausted.' Chinese has thousands of these four-character set phrases. Other fatigue chéngyǔ: 精疲力竭 (jīngpí lìjié, the same meaning), 心力交瘁 (xīnlì jiāocuì, mentally and physically drained). They appear frequently in formal and written Chinese.

sentences.base_language: A doctor examined him and asked: "Do you exercise? How is your diet?"
sentences.target_language: 一位医生给他检查了身体，问道：“您平时锻炼吗？饮食怎么样？”
sentences.romanized: Yī wèi yīshēng gěi tā jiǎnchá le shēntǐ, wèn dào: “Nín píngshí duànliàn ma? Yǐnshí zěnme yàng?”
sentences.grammarNote:
**给他检查** = examine for him — the **给** (gěi) coverb can express 'for/on behalf of' or 'to': 给他**检查** (examine for him), 给我**解释** (explain to me). This **给** introduces the person affected by the action, different from **给** meaning 'to give.'
**饮食怎么样** = how is your diet? — **饮食** (yǐnshí) = diet/food and drink: **饮** (yǐn, to drink) + **食** (shí, food). A medical/formal term. Everyday: 你平时吃什么? (what do you usually eat?). **怎么样** (zěnme yàng) = how is ~ / what is ~ like? — a versatile question: 身体怎么样? (how's your health?), 工作怎么样? (how's work?)

sentences.base_language: He admitted he had been eating a lot of junk food and barely exercising.
sentences.target_language: 他承认自己一直在吃很多垃圾食品，几乎没有锻炼。
sentences.romanized: Tā chéngrèn zìjǐ yīzhí zài chī hěn duō lājī shípǐn, jīhū méiyǒu duànliàn.
sentences.grammarNote:
**一直在 + verb** = have been (continuously) doing — **一直** (yīzhí, continuously/all along) + **在** (ongoing aspect) + verb: 他**一直在**工作 (he's been working continuously). This double marker (**一直** + **在**) strongly emphasises ongoing, uninterrupted action.
**几乎没有** = barely/almost not: **几乎** (jīhū, almost/nearly) + **没有** (méiyǒu, not have/not): 几乎没有锻炼 (barely exercised), 几乎没有睡觉 (barely slept). **垃圾食品** (lājī shípǐn) = junk food: **垃圾** (lājī, garbage/rubbish) + **食品** (shípǐn, food products). The same compound logic as in English.

sentences.base_language: The doctor said: "You need more rest and a better diet. Drink more water."
sentences.target_language: 医生说：“您需要多休息，改善饮食，还要多喝水。”
sentences.romanized: Yīshēng shuō: “Nín xūyào duō xiūxi, gǎishàn yǐnshí, hái yào duō hē shuǐ.”
sentences.grammarNote:
**多 + verb** = do more of (an action): **多**休息 (rest more), **多**喝水 (drink more water), **多**锻炼 (exercise more). **多** placed before a verb directly gives this meaning of 'do more of.' Compare: **少** + verb (do less of): **少**吃糖 (eat less sugar), **少**喝酒 (drink less alcohol).
**改善** (gǎishàn) = improve — a formal verb: 改善**飞食** (improve one's diet), 改善**生活** (improve one's life). More colloquial alternatives: 变好 (become better), 提高 (raise/improve — for levels or standards). **还要** = also need to / furthermore — **还** (hái, still/also) + **要** (yào, need/must).

sentences.base_language: He felt a little embarrassed, but also relieved to hear there was nothing serious.
sentences.target_language: 他感到有些尴尬，但听到没什么大问题，心里也松了一口气。
sentences.romanized: Tā gǎndào yǒuxiē gāngà, dàn tīng dào méi shénme dà wèntí, xīnlǐ yě sōng le yī kǒu qì.
literal: also breathed a sigh of relief
sentences.grammarNote:
**松了一口气** = breathed a sigh of relief, lit. 'loosened a breath' — a common idiom: **松** (sōng, loosen/relax) + **一口气** (yī kǒu qì, one breath). A vivid physical metaphor for relief. Similar: **放下心来** (let the heart settle = feel relieved), **如释重负** (like putting down a heavy load — chéngyǔ for relief).
**有些** = somewhat/a bit: **有些**尴尬 (a bit embarrassed), **有些**累 (a bit tired). **有些** is interchangeable with **有点** (yǒudiǎn) in most cases, but **有些** is slightly more formal or written. **尴尬** (gāngà) = embarrassed/awkward — a common adjective that appears in many contexts.

sentences.base_language: He left the hospital and found a grocery store nearby.
sentences.target_language: 他离开了医院，在附近找到了一家超市。
sentences.romanized: Tā líkāi le yīyuàn, zài fùjìn zhǎodào le yī jiā chāoshì.
sentences.grammarNote:
**离开** (líkāi) = to leave/depart — **离** (lí, separate) + **开** (kāi, open/away). The directional complement **开** adds a sense of 'away from': 跑**开** (run away), 走**开** (walk away), 离**开** (leave). Compare **出** (chū, exit): **出**医院 would mean 'exit the hospital building'.
**找到了** = found (successfully) — resultative complement: **找** (zhǎo, look for) + **到** (dào, reach/arrive at) = successfully find. Compare: **找** (looking for, no result), **找到** (found it — result achieved). The **了** confirms completion: **找到了** (found it — done). This 到 as a resultative complement is extremely frequent: 做**到** (accomplish), 听**到** (hear), 看**到** (see/catch sight of).

sentences.base_language: He bought vegetables, fish, and fruit — no snacks this time.
sentences.target_language: 他买了蔬菜、鱼和水果——这次没买零食。
sentences.romanized: Tā mǎile shūcài, yú hé shuǐguǒ — zhè cì méi mǎi língshí.
sentences.grammarNote:
**这次** = this time — **次** (cì) is the measure word for occurrences/times: 这**次** (this time), 上**次** (last time), 下**次** (next time), 每**次** (every time), 第一**次** (the first time). **次** is one of the most useful measure words in Chinese — you'll use it constantly: 我去过三**次** (I've been there three times).
**零食** (língshí) = snacks — **零** (líng, zero/scattered/miscellaneous) + **食** (shí, food): miscellaneous nibbling food. The character **零** captures the idea of scattered little things. Other **零** words: **零钱** (líng qián, change/small change), **零件** (língjiàn, spare parts).

sentences.base_language: Back home, he made a simple soup with vegetables and chicken.
sentences.target_language: 回到家，他用蔬菜和鸡肉煮了一锅简单的汤。
sentences.romanized: Huí dào jiā, tā yòng shūcài hé jīròu zhǔ le yī guō jiǎndān de tāng.
sentences.grammarNote:
**回到** = return to — directional complement **到** confirms reaching the destination: 回**到**家 (returned home — successfully got back). Compare: 回家 (go/come home — direction, not necessarily arrived): 他要回家 (he wants to go home) vs 他回**到**家了 (he has returned home — completed).
**用** (yòng) = using/with — the coverb **用** introduces the instrument or material: **用**蔬菜煮汤 (make soup with vegetables), **用**筷子吃饭 (eat with chopsticks), **用**手写 (write by hand). **用** + noun + verb is the standard pattern for describing means. **一锅** (yī guō) = one pot — **锅** (guō) is the measure word for pot-cooked dishes.

sentences.base_language: While cooking, he called his friend and told her about the hospital visit.
sentences.target_language: 做饭的时候，他给朋友打了电话，告诉她去医院的事。
sentences.romanized: Zuòfàn de shíhou, tā gěi péngyou dǎ le diànhuà, gàosu tā qù yīyuàn de shì.
literal: the matter of going to hospital
sentences.grammarNote:
**做饭的时候** = while cooking, lit. 'at the time of making food' — **的时候** (de shíhou, at the time of) after a verb phrase creates a temporal clause: 睡觉**的时候** (while sleeping), 下雨**的时候** (when it rains). Very natural and widely used in Chinese.
**告诉** (gàosu) = to tell — takes two objects: 告诉[person][information]: 告诉**她**[去医院的事] (told **her** [about the hospital visit]). **去医院的事** = the matter of going to the hospital: **事** (shì, matter/thing/affair) nominalises the preceding phrase. Compare: **去医院这件事** (this matter of going to the hospital) — same meaning with 这件 added.

sentences.base_language: His friend laughed and said: "I always told you to take care of yourself!"
sentences.target_language: 朋友笑着说：“我早就告诉过你要注意身体嘛！”
sentences.romanized: Péngyou xiào zhe shuō: “Wǒ zǎo jiù gàosu guò nǐ yào zhùyì shēntǐ ma!”
sentences.grammarNote:
**笑着说** = said laughing — **V + 着** (zhe) creates a simultaneous manner: 笑**着**说 (said while laughing), 站**着**看 (watched while standing), 跑**着**回家 (ran home). **着** marks an accompanying or simultaneous action/state.
**早就告诉过** — **过** (guò) marks past experience: 我告诉**过**你 (I have told you [before]). This **过** is the experience marker, emphasising that the action happened previously — implying 'you should have known.' **早就** (zǎo jiù) = long ago already: 我**早就**知道了 (I knew that long ago). **嘛** (ma) is a sentence-final particle expressing 'obviously/as expected': 当然**嘛** (of course!).

sentences.base_language: After eating, he felt much better — his energy was gradually returning.
sentences.target_language: 吃完饭后，他感觉好多了——精力也慢慢恢复了。
sentences.romanized: Chī wán fàn hòu, tā gǎnjué hǎo duō le — jīnglì yě màn màn huīfù le.
sentences.grammarNote:
**好多了** = much better — **好** + **多** + **了**: 好 (good/well) + 多 (much/more) + 了 (change-of-state particle). This is the standard way to express 'much better': 身体好多了 (feeling much better), 天气好多了 (the weather is much better). Compare: **好一些** (a little better — smaller improvement).
**慢慢** = gradually/slowly — reduplication of **慢** (màn, slow): **慢慢**地 or simply **慢慢** as an adverb: **慢慢**走 (walk slowly), **慢慢**来 (take it slowly / no rush — a common reassuring phrase). **恢复** (huīfù) = recover/restore: 精力**恢复** (energy recovers), **恢复**健康 (recover one's health).

sentences.base_language: He lay down and rested for a few hours.
sentences.target_language: 他躺下来休息了几个小时。
sentences.romanized: Tā tǎng xià lái xiūxi le jǐ gè xiǎoshí.
sentences.grammarNote:
**躺下来** = lie down — **躺** (tǎng, to lie/recline) + **下来** (xià lái, down + come = directional complement). **下来** as a directional complement expresses moving to a lower or settled position: 坐**下来** (sit down), 停**下来** (stop/come to a halt), 冷静**下来** (calm down). The **来** suggests movement toward the speaker's perspective.
**几个小时** = a few hours — **几** (jǐ, how many/several) + **个** (gè, general measure word) + **小时** (xiǎoshí, hour). **几** as an indefinite number means 'a few' (small unspecified number): 几**天** (a few days), 几**个人** (a few people). Compare: **一些** (yīxiē, some — can be larger) and **几** (specifically 'a few/several, not many').

sentences.base_language: By evening, his mood had completely improved and he felt calm and content.
sentences.target_language: 到了晚上，他的心情完全好了，感到平静又满足。
sentences.romanized: Dào le wǎnshang, tā de xīnqíng wánquán hǎo le, gǎndào píngjìng yòu mǎnzú.
sentences.grammarNote:
**到了晚上** = by evening / when evening came — **到了** (dào le) + time: once a time is reached. Compare: **晚上到了** (evening arrived) vs **到了晚上** (by/when evening came). **到了** + time is a natural way to set the scene: **到了**下午 (by afternoon), **到了**周末 (come the weekend).
**又...又...** = both...and... — a parallel structure showing two simultaneous qualities: 平静**又**满足 (both calm and content). More emphatic than using **和**: 平静和满足 (calm and content). **又A又B** strongly links the two: 又快**又**好 (both fast and good), 又高**又**瘦 (both tall and thin).

sentences.base_language: He told himself that from now on he would take good care of his body.
sentences.target_language: 他告诉自己，从现在起要好好爱护自己的身体。
sentences.romanized: Tā gàosu zìjǐ, cóng xiànzài qǐ yào hǎohǎo àihù zìjǐ de shēntǐ.
sentences.grammarNote:
**从现在起** = from now on — **从** (cóng, from) + **现在** (xiànzài, now) + **起** (qǐ, starting point): from now as a starting point. Similar: **从今以后** (cóng jīn yǐhòu, from now onward — more formal/literary), **以后** (from now on / afterward — simpler). These temporal anchors are essential for expressing future intentions.
**爱护** (àihù) = to cherish/take good care of — **爱** (ài, love) + **护** (hù, protect/guard): to love and guard. More affectionate and careful than just **保护** (bǎohù, protect) or **照顾** (zhàogù, take care of). **爱护身体** implies both love for and careful maintenance of one's body — a common self-care phrase.
